Using over one million articles published between January 2023 and June 2024, we automatically detect interrogative stances, approximate their functional types, and locate textual answers when present, linking these quantitative measures to a qualitatively annotated subcorpus grounded in semantic and pragmatic theories of questions. Interrogatives are sparse but systematically patterned: they mainly introduce or organize issues, with most remaining cases being information-seeking or echo-like, while explicitly leading or tag questions are rare. Although their density and mix vary across outlets and topics, our heuristic suggests that questions are overwhelmingly taken up within the same article and usually linked to a subsequent answer-like span, most often in the journalist's narrative voice and less often through quoted speech. Interrogative contexts are densely populated with named individuals, organizations, and places, whereas publics and broad social groups are mentioned much less frequently, suggesting that interrogative discourse tends to foreground already prominent actors and places and thus exhibits strong personalization. We show how interrogative stance, textual uptake, and voice can be operationalized at corpus scale, and argue that combining computational methods with pragmatic and sociological perspectives can help account for how questioning practices structure contemporary news discourse.

AI.tldr: Large-scale computational analysis of interrogatives in French digital news reveals systematic patterns: questions are sparse but function mainly to introduce/organize issues, are usually answered within articles, and foreground prominent actors through personalization.
AI.motivation: Previous research has examined interrogatives primarily in broadcast interviews and small English corpora, while large-scale computational studies of news rarely distinguish interrogatives from declaratives or analyze their functions. This study aims to bridge linguistics, conversation analysis, and computational approaches to understand how questioning practices structure contemporary news discourse.
AI.method: Mixed-methods study analyzing over one million French-language digital news articles (Jan 2023-Jun 2024). Combines automatic detection of interrogative stances, functional type approximation, and location of textual answers with qualitative annotation of a subcorpus grounded in semantic and pragmatic theories of questions.
AI.result: Interrogatives are sparse but systematically patterned: mainly introduce/organize issues, with most remaining cases being information-seeking or echo-like; explicitly leading or tag questions are rare. Questions are overwhelmingly answered within the same article, usually in journalist's narrative voice. Interrogative contexts densely feature named individuals, organizations, and places, while publics and broad social groups are mentioned less frequently, indicating strong personalization.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates how interrogative stance, textual uptake, and voice can be operationalized at corpus scale, and argues that combining computational methods with pragmatic and sociological perspectives helps account for how questioning practices structure contemporary news discourse, revealing systematic patterns of personalization and issue organization.

title: Hierarchical Text-Guided Brain Tumor Segmentation via Sub-Region-Aware Prompts
summary: Brain tumor segmentation remains challenging because the three standard sub-regions, i.e., whole tumor (WT), tumor core (TC), and enhancing tumor (ET), often exhibit ambiguous visual boundaries. Integrating radiological description texts with imaging has shown promise. However, most multimodal approaches typically compress a report into a single global text embedding shared across all sub-regions, overlooking their distinct clinical characteristics. We propose TextCSP (text-modulated soft cascade architecture), a hierarchical text-guided framework that builds on the TextBraTS baseline with three novel components: (1) a text-modulated soft cascade decoder that predicts WT->TC->ET in a coarse-to-fine manner consistent with their anatomical containment hierarchy. (2) sub-region-aware prompt tuning, which uses learnable soft prompts with a LoRA-adapted BioBERT encoder to generate specialized text representations tailored for each sub-region; (3) text-semantic channel modulators that convert the aforementioned representations into channel-wise refinement signals, enabling the decoder to emphasize features aligned with clinically described patterns. Experiments on the TextBraTS dataset demonstrate consistent improvements across all sub-regions against state-of-the-art methods by 1.7% and 6% on the main metrics Dice and HD95.

AI.tldr: TextCSP is a hierarchical text-guided framework for brain tumor segmentation that uses text-modulated soft cascade decoding, sub-region-aware prompt tuning, and text-semantic channel modulators to improve segmentation of whole tumor, tumor core, and enhancing tumor regions.
AI.motivation: Brain tumor segmentation is challenging due to ambiguous visual boundaries between the three standard sub-regions (WT, TC, ET). Current multimodal approaches typically compress radiological reports into single global embeddings shared across all sub-regions, overlooking their distinct clinical characteristics.
AI.method: TextCSP builds on TextBraTS baseline with three components: (1) text-modulated soft cascade decoder predicting WT→TC→ET in coarse-to-fine anatomical hierarchy, (2) sub-region-aware prompt tuning using learnable soft prompts with LoRA-adapted BioBERT encoder for specialized text representations per sub-region, (3) text-semantic channel modulators converting text representations into channel-wise refinement signals to emphasize clinically described patterns.
AI.result: Experiments on TextBraTS dataset show consistent improvements across all sub-regions against state-of-the-art methods by 1.7% on Dice and 6% on HD95 metrics.
AI.conclusion: TextCSP effectively integrates radiological text descriptions with imaging through hierarchical text guidance, addressing limitations of global text embeddings and improving brain tumor segmentation performance by leveraging distinct clinical characteristics of each sub-region.

title: Pruned Adaptation Modules: A Simple yet Strong Baseline for Continual Foundation Models
summary: The continual learning literature has rapidly shifted from traditional class incremental learning (CIL) techniques to foundation model (FM)-based CIL methods without a clear understanding of how these newer approaches compare to strong, lightweight convolutional baselines. This abrupt transition has created a substantial methodological gap, making it difficult to assess whether recent FM-based CIL progress reflects genuine advances or merely the absence of rigorous baselines. To address this gap, we introduce Pruned Adaptation Modules (PAM), a simple yet effective method that freezes the vast majority of the pre-trained ResNet while enabling scalable continual adaptation through sparse task-specific layers. PAM yields up to a ~5x reduction in trainable parameters and a ~6x reduction in total parameters, significantly reducing the cost of continual updates. Across diverse benchmarks, PAM consistently mitigates catastrophic forgetting and outperforms state-of-the-art FM-based CIL approaches. Our findings position PAM as a strong and transparent baseline that helps bridge the gap between traditional and FM-based CIL, guiding future research for a more accurate assessment of true progress in continual adaptation. The code can be found at: https://github.com/ElifCerenGokYildirim/PAM.

AI.tldr: PAM introduces a lightweight continual learning method using pruned adaptation modules that freezes most of a pre-trained ResNet, achieving superior performance with significantly fewer parameters than FM-based approaches.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the methodological gap in continual learning research where the field has shifted from traditional CIL techniques to FM-based methods without proper comparison to strong convolutional baselines, making it difficult to assess whether recent progress represents genuine advances or just the absence of rigorous baselines.
AI.method: PAM (Pruned Adaptation Modules) freezes the vast majority of a pre-trained ResNet backbone and enables scalable continual adaptation through sparse task-specific layers. This approach minimizes trainable parameters while maintaining effective continual learning capabilities.
AI.result: PAM achieves up to ~5x reduction in trainable parameters and ~6x reduction in total parameters compared to existing methods. It consistently mitigates catastrophic forgetting and outperforms state-of-the-art FM-based CIL approaches across diverse benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: PAM serves as a strong, transparent baseline that bridges the gap between traditional and FM-based CIL methods, providing a more accurate framework for assessing true progress in continual adaptation research.

title: Guideline-grounded retrieval-augmented generation for ophthalmic clinical decision support
summary: In this work, we propose Oph-Guid-RAG, a multimodal visual RAG system for ophthalmology clinical question answering and decision support. We treat each guideline page as an independent evidence unit and directly retrieve page images, preserving tables, flowcharts, and layout information. We further design a controllable retrieval framework with routing and filtering, which selectively introduces external evidence and reduces noise. The system integrates query decomposition, query rewriting, retrieval, reranking, and multimodal reasoning, and provides traceable outputs with guideline page references. We evaluate our method on HealthBench using a doctor-based scoring protocol. On the hard subset, our approach improves the overall score from 0.2969 to 0.3861 (+0.0892, +30.0%) compared to GPT-5.2, and achieves higher accuracy, improving from 0.5956 to 0.6576 (+0.0620, +10.4%). Compared to GPT-5.4, our method achieves a larger accuracy gain of +0.1289 (+24.4%). These results show that our method is more effective on challenging cases that require precise, evidence-based reasoning. Ablation studies further show that reranking, routing, and retrieval design are critical for stable performance, especially under difficult settings. Overall, we show how combining visionbased retrieval with controllable reasoning can improve evidence grounding and robustness in clinical AI applications,while pointing out that further work is needed to be more complete.

AI.tldr: Oph-Guid-RAG is a multimodal visual retrieval-augmented generation system for ophthalmology clinical QA that retrieves guideline page images as evidence units and uses controllable retrieval with routing/filtering to improve evidence-based reasoning.
AI.motivation: To address the need for precise, evidence-based clinical decision support in ophthalmology by preserving visual information (tables, flowcharts, layout) from guideline pages and reducing noise in retrieval-augmented generation systems.
AI.method: Treats each guideline page as independent evidence unit; retrieves page images directly; implements controllable retrieval framework with routing and filtering; integrates query decomposition, rewriting, retrieval, reranking, and multimodal reasoning; provides traceable outputs with page references.
AI.result: On HealthBench hard subset: improves overall score from 0.2969 to 0.3861 (+30.0%) vs GPT-5.2; accuracy improves from 0.5956 to 0.6576 (+10.4%) vs GPT-5.2; achieves +0.1289 (+24.4%) accuracy gain vs GPT-5.4; ablation shows reranking, routing, and retrieval design critical for stable performance.
AI.conclusion: Combining vision-based retrieval with controllable reasoning improves evidence grounding and robustness in clinical AI applications; shows effectiveness on challenging cases requiring precise evidence-based reasoning; further work needed for completeness.

title: Instruction Set and Language for Symbolic Regression
summary: A fundamental but largely unaddressed obstacle in Symbolic regression (SR) is structural redundancy: every expression DAG with admits many distinct node-numbering schemes that all encode the same expression, each occupying a separate point in the search space and consuming fitness evaluations without adding diversity. We present IsalSR (Instruction Set and Language for Symbolic Regression), a representation framework that encodes expression DAGs as strings over a compact two-tier alphabet and computes a pruned canonical string -- a complete labeled-DAG isomorphism invariant -- that collapses all the equivalent representations into a single canonical form.

AI.tldr: IsalSR introduces a canonical representation for symbolic regression that eliminates structural redundancy by collapsing equivalent expression DAGs into a single canonical form.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the fundamental problem of structural redundancy in symbolic regression, where multiple distinct node-numbering schemes encode the same expression, wasting fitness evaluations and search space without adding diversity.
AI.method: IsalSR uses a representation framework that encodes expression DAGs as strings over a compact two-tier alphabet and computes a pruned canonical string that serves as a complete labeled-DAG isomorphism invariant.
AI.result: The method collapses all equivalent representations of the same expression into a single canonical form, eliminating structural redundancy in symbolic regression search spaces.
AI.conclusion: IsalSR provides a solution to structural redundancy in symbolic regression by introducing canonical representations that improve search efficiency by avoiding wasted evaluations on equivalent expression encodings.

title: Taming Sampling Perturbations with Variance Expansion Loss for Latent Diffusion Models
summary: Latent diffusion models have emerged as the dominant framework for high-fidelity and efficient image generation, owing to their ability to learn diffusion processes in compact latent spaces. However, while previous research has focused primarily on reconstruction accuracy and semantic alignment of the latent space, we observe that another critical factor, robustness to sampling perturbations, also plays a crucial role in determining generation quality. Through empirical and theoretical analyses, we show that the commonly used $β$-VAE-based tokenizers in latent diffusion models, tend to produce overly compact latent manifolds that are highly sensitive to stochastic perturbations during diffusion sampling, leading to visual degradation. To address this issue, we propose a simple yet effective solution that constructs a latent space robust to sampling perturbations while maintaining strong reconstruction fidelity. This is achieved by introducing a Variance Expansion loss that counteracts variance collapse and leverages the adversarial interplay between reconstruction and variance expansion to achieve an adaptive balance that preserves reconstruction accuracy while improving robustness to stochastic sampling. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach consistently enhances generation quality across different latent diffusion architectures, confirming that robustness in latent space is a key missing ingredient for stable and faithful diffusion sampling.

AI.tldr: Latent diffusion models suffer from sensitivity to sampling perturbations due to overly compact latent spaces from β-VAE tokenizers; proposed Variance Expansion loss improves robustness while maintaining reconstruction fidelity.
AI.motivation: Previous research focused on reconstruction accuracy and semantic alignment, but overlooked robustness to sampling perturbations as a critical factor for generation quality. β-VAE-based tokenizers create overly compact latent manifolds that are highly sensitive to stochastic perturbations during diffusion sampling, leading to visual degradation.
AI.method: Proposed Variance Expansion loss that counteracts variance collapse in latent space. Leverages adversarial interplay between reconstruction and variance expansion to achieve adaptive balance, preserving reconstruction accuracy while improving robustness to stochastic sampling.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent enhancement of generation quality across different latent diffusion architectures. Confirms that robustness in latent space is a key missing ingredient for stable and faithful diffusion sampling.
AI.conclusion: Robustness to sampling perturbations is crucial for latent diffusion models; the proposed Variance Expansion loss effectively addresses this by creating latent spaces that maintain reconstruction fidelity while being robust to stochastic perturbations, leading to improved generation quality.

title: Rethinking Plasticity in Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: This paper investigates the fundamental mechanisms driving plasticity loss in deep reinforcement learning (RL), a critical challenge where neural networks lose their ability to adapt to non-stationary environments. While existing research often relies on descriptive metrics like dormant neurons or effective rank, these summaries fail to explain the underlying optimization dynamics. We propose the Optimization-Centric Plasticity (OCP) hypothesis, which posits that plasticity loss arises because optimal points from previous tasks become poor local optima for new tasks, trapping parameters during task transitions and hindering subsequent learning. We theoretically establish the equivalence between neuron dormancy and zero-gradient states, demonstrating that the absence of gradient signals is the primary driver of dormancy. Our experiments reveal that plasticity loss is highly task-specific; notably, networks with high dormancy rates in one task can achieve performance parity with randomly initialized networks when switched to a significantly different task, suggesting that the network's capacity remains intact but is inhibited by the specific optimization landscape. Furthermore, our hypothesis elucidates why parameter constraints mitigate plasticity loss by preventing deep entrenchment in local optima. Validated across diverse non-stationary scenarios, our findings provide a rigorous optimization-based framework for understanding and restoring network plasticity in complex RL domains.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes the Optimization-Centric Plasticity (OCP) hypothesis, explaining plasticity loss in deep RL as parameters getting trapped in poor local optima from previous tasks, preventing adaptation to new tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing research on plasticity loss in deep RL relies on descriptive metrics like dormant neurons or effective rank, which fail to explain the underlying optimization dynamics driving the loss of adaptability in non-stationary environments.
AI.method: Proposes the OCP hypothesis linking plasticity loss to optimization dynamics, theoretically establishes equivalence between neuron dormancy and zero-gradient states, and conducts experiments across diverse non-stationary scenarios to validate the hypothesis.
AI.result: Plasticity loss is task-specific; networks with high dormancy in one task can achieve performance parity with randomly initialized networks on significantly different tasks. Parameter constraints mitigate plasticity loss by preventing deep entrenchment in local optima.
AI.conclusion: The OCP hypothesis provides a rigorous optimization-based framework for understanding and restoring network plasticity in complex RL domains, explaining why existing descriptive metrics fail and offering insights for developing more adaptive RL systems.

title: Future-Interactions-Aware Trajectory Prediction via Braid Theory
comment: To be published in IEEE Intelligent Vehicles Symposium (IV) 2026
summary: To safely operate, an autonomous vehicle must know the future behavior of a potentially high number of interacting agents around it, a task often posed as multi-agent trajectory prediction. Many previous attempts to model social interactions and solve the joint prediction task either add extensive computational requirements or rely on heuristics to label multi-agent behavior types. Braid theory, in contrast, provides a powerful exact descriptor of multi-agent behavior by projecting future trajectories into braids that express how trajectories cross with each other over time; a braid then corresponds to a specific mode of coordination between the multiple agents in the future. In past work, braids have been used lightly to reason about interacting agents and restrict the attention window of predicted agents. We show that leveraging more fully the expressivity of the braid representation and using it to condition the trajectories themselves leads to even further gains in joint prediction performance, with negligible added complexity either in training or at inference time. We do so by proposing a novel auxiliary task, braid prediction, done in parallel with the trajectory prediction task. By classifying edges between agents into their correct crossing types in the braid representation, the braid prediction task is able to imbue the model with improved social awareness, which is reflected in joint predictions that more closely adhere to the actual multi-agent behavior. This simple auxiliary task allowed us to obtain significant improvements in joint metrics on three separate datasets. We show how the braid prediction task infuses the model with future intention awareness, leading to more accurate joint predictions. Code is available at github.com/caiocj1/traj-pred-braid-theory.

AI.tldr: Braid theory provides exact descriptors of multi-agent behavior by projecting trajectories into braids that express crossing patterns; using braid prediction as an auxiliary task improves joint trajectory prediction performance with minimal computational overhead.
AI.motivation: Current multi-agent trajectory prediction methods either require extensive computational resources or rely on heuristic labeling of behavior types. There's a need for more efficient and mathematically rigorous approaches to model social interactions between multiple agents for autonomous vehicle safety.
AI.method: Proposes a novel auxiliary task of braid prediction done in parallel with trajectory prediction. Classifies edges between agents into correct crossing types in the braid representation, which encodes how trajectories cross over time. This imbues the model with improved social awareness without adding significant computational complexity during training or inference.
AI.result: Significant improvements in joint metrics on three separate datasets. The braid prediction task infuses the model with future intention awareness, leading to more accurate joint predictions that better adhere to actual multi-agent behavior.
AI.conclusion: Leveraging the full expressivity of braid theory as an auxiliary conditioning mechanism substantially enhances multi-agent trajectory prediction performance with negligible added complexity, providing a mathematically rigorous approach to modeling social interactions for autonomous systems.

title: Select, Label, Evaluate: Active Testing in NLP
summary: Human annotation cost and time remain significant bottlenecks in Natural Language Processing (NLP), with test data annotation being particularly expensive due to the stringent requirement for low-error and high-quality labels necessary for reliable model evaluation. Traditional approaches require annotating entire test sets, leading to substantial resource requirements. Active Testing is a framework that selects the most informative test samples for annotation. Given a labeling budget, it aims to choose the subset that best estimates model performance while minimizing cost and human effort. In this work, we formalize Active Testing in NLP and we conduct an extensive benchmarking of existing approaches across 18 datasets and 4 embedding strategies spanning 4 different NLP tasks. The experiments show annotation reductions of up to 95%, with performance estimation accuracy difference from the full test set within 1%. Our analysis reveals variations in method effectiveness across different data characteristics and task types, with no single approach emerging as universally superior. Lastly, to address the limitation of requiring a predefined annotation budget in existing sample selection strategies, we introduce an adaptive stopping criterion that automatically determines the optimal number of samples.

AI.tldr: Active Testing framework reduces NLP test annotation costs by up to 95% through intelligent sample selection, with performance estimation accuracy within 1% of full test sets.
AI.motivation: Human annotation cost and time are major bottlenecks in NLP, especially for test data which requires high-quality labels for reliable model evaluation. Traditional approaches requiring full test set annotation are resource-intensive.
AI.method: Formalizes Active Testing framework for NLP, benchmarks existing approaches across 18 datasets and 4 embedding strategies spanning 4 NLP tasks, and introduces adaptive stopping criterion to automatically determine optimal sample size.
AI.result: Achieves annotation reductions of up to 95% while maintaining performance estimation accuracy within 1% difference from full test sets. Analysis shows method effectiveness varies across data characteristics and task types, with no single universally superior approach.
AI.conclusion: Active Testing provides significant cost savings for NLP evaluation while maintaining reliable performance estimation. The adaptive stopping criterion addresses limitations of predefined budgets, and method selection should consider task and data characteristics.

title: DGRNet: Disagreement-Guided Refinement for Uncertainty-Aware Brain Tumor Segmentation
summary: Accurate brain tumor segmentation from MRI scans is critical for diagnosis and treatment planning. Despite the strong performance of recent deep learning approaches, two fundamental limitations remain: (1) the lack of reliable uncertainty quantification in single-model predictions, which is essential for clinical deployment because the level of uncertainty may impact treatment decision-making, and (2) the under-utilization of rich information in radiology reports that can guide segmentation in ambiguous regions. In this paper, we propose the Disagreement-Guided Refinement Network (DGRNet), a novel framework that addresses both limitations through multi-view disagreement-based uncertainty estimation and text-conditioned refinement. DGRNet generates diverse predictions via four lightweight view-specific adapters attached to a shared encoder-decoder, enabling efficient uncertainty quantification within a single forward pass. Afterward, we build disagreement maps to identify regions of high segmentation uncertainty, which are then selectively refined according to clinical reports. Moreover, we introduce a diversity-preserving training strategy that combines pairwise similarity penalties and gradient isolation to prevent view collapse. The experimental results on the TextBraTS dataset show that DGRNet favorably improves state-of-the-art segmentation accuracy by 2.4% and 11% in main metrics Dice and HD95, respectively, while providing meaningful uncertainty estimates.

AI.tldr: DGRNet is a brain tumor segmentation framework that addresses uncertainty quantification and text-guided refinement using multi-view disagreement and clinical report conditioning.
AI.motivation: Two key limitations in current brain tumor segmentation: (1) lack of reliable uncertainty quantification in single-model predictions for clinical decision-making, and (2) under-utilization of radiology report information that could guide segmentation in ambiguous regions.
AI.method: Disagreement-Guided Refinement Network (DGRNet) uses four lightweight view-specific adapters attached to a shared encoder-decoder to generate diverse predictions in a single forward pass. It builds disagreement maps to identify high-uncertainty regions, then selectively refines them using clinical reports. Includes diversity-preserving training with pairwise similarity penalties and gradient isolation to prevent view collapse.
AI.result: On TextBraTS dataset, DGRNet improves state-of-the-art segmentation accuracy by 2.4% in Dice and 11% in HD95 metrics while providing meaningful uncertainty estimates.
AI.conclusion: DGRNet effectively addresses both uncertainty quantification and text-guided refinement challenges in brain tumor segmentation, demonstrating significant improvements in accuracy metrics while providing clinically useful uncertainty information.

title: Reward Sharpness-Aware Fine-Tuning for Diffusion Models
summary: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) has proven effective in aligning large language models with human preferences, inspiring the development of reward-centric diffusion reinforcement learning (RDRL) to achieve similar alignment and controllability. While diffusion models can generate high-quality outputs, RDRL remains susceptible to reward hacking, where the reward score increases without corresponding improvements in perceptual quality. We demonstrate that this vulnerability arises from the non-robustness of reward model gradients, particularly when the reward landscape with respect to the input image is sharp. To mitigate this issue, we introduce methods that exploit gradients from a robustified reward model without requiring its retraining. Specifically, we employ gradients from a flattened reward model, obtained through parameter perturbations of the diffusion model and perturbations of its generated samples. Empirically, each method independently alleviates reward hacking and improves robustness, while their joint use amplifies these benefits. Our resulting framework, RSA-FT (Reward Sharpness-Aware Fine-Tuning), is simple, broadly compatible, and consistently enhances the reliability of RDRL.

AI.tldr: RSA-FT framework addresses reward hacking in diffusion RL by using robustified reward model gradients without retraining, improving alignment reliability.
AI.motivation: Reward-centric diffusion reinforcement learning (RDRL) suffers from reward hacking where reward scores increase without perceptual quality improvements, due to non-robust reward model gradients in sharp reward landscapes.
AI.method: Introduces RSA-FT (Reward Sharpness-Aware Fine-Tuning) that exploits gradients from robustified reward models without retraining, using parameter perturbations of diffusion models and perturbations of generated samples to obtain flattened reward gradients.
AI.result: Each method independently alleviates reward hacking and improves robustness; joint use amplifies benefits. RSA-FT is simple, broadly compatible, and consistently enhances RDRL reliability.
AI.conclusion: The proposed RSA-FT framework effectively mitigates reward hacking in diffusion reinforcement learning by leveraging robustified reward gradients, providing a practical solution for improving alignment and controllability without requiring reward model retraining.

title: A Context Engineering Framework for Improving Enterprise AI Agents based on Digital-Twin MDP
summary: Despite rapid progress in AI agents for enterprise automation and decision-making, their real-world deployment and further performance gains remain constrained by limited data quality and quantity, complex real-world reasoning demands, difficulties with self-play, and the lack of reliable feedback signals. To address these challenges, we propose a lightweight, model-agnostic framework for improving LLM-based enterprise agents via offline reinforcement learning (RL). The proposed Context Engineering via DT-MDP (DT-MDP-CE) framework comprises three key components: (1) A Digital-Twin Markov Decision Process (DT-MDP), which abstracts the agent's reasoning behavior as a finite MDP; (2) A robust contrastive inverse RL, which, armed with the DT-MDP, to efficiently estimate a well-founded reward function and induces policies from mixed-quality offline trajectories; and (3) RL-guided context engineering, which uses the policy obtained from the integrated process of (1) and (2), to improve the agent's decision-making behavior. As a case study, we apply the framework to a representative task in the enterprise-oriented domain of IT automation. Extensive experimental results demonstrate consistent and significant improvements over baseline agents across a wide range of evaluation settings, suggesting that the framework can generalize to other agents sharing similar characteristics in enterprise environments.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, model-agnostic framework called DT-MDP-CE improves LLM-based enterprise agents using offline reinforcement learning, addressing data limitations and complex reasoning demands through digital-twin MDP abstraction, contrastive inverse RL, and RL-guided context engineering.
AI.motivation: Real-world deployment of AI agents for enterprise automation is constrained by limited data quality/quantity, complex reasoning demands, difficulties with self-play, and lack of reliable feedback signals, necessitating improved agent performance.
AI.method: Proposes DT-MDP-CE framework with three components: (1) Digital-Twin Markov Decision Process (DT-MDP) abstracting agent reasoning as finite MDP; (2) Robust contrastive inverse RL to estimate reward functions from mixed-quality offline trajectories; (3) RL-guided context engineering using derived policies to improve agent decision-making.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on IT automation tasks demonstrate consistent and significant improvements over baseline agents across wide evaluation settings, suggesting framework generalizability to similar enterprise agents.
AI.conclusion: The lightweight, model-agnostic DT-MDP-CE framework effectively addresses enterprise agent limitations through offline RL, enabling improved decision-making and potential generalization to various enterprise automation scenarios.

title: Riding Brainwaves in LLM Space: Understanding Activation Patterns Using Individual Neural Signatures
summary: Consumer-grade EEG is entering everyday devices, from earbuds to headbands, raising the question of whether language models can be adapted to individual neural responses. We test this by asking whether frozen LLM representations encode person-specific EEG signals, directions in activation space that predict one person's brain activity but not another's. Using word-level EEG from 30 participants reading naturalistic sentences (ZuCo corpus), we train a separate linear probe for each person, mapping hidden states from a frozen Qwen 2.5 7B to that individual's EEG power. Person-specific probes outperform a single population probe on every EEG feature tested; for high-gamma power, the person-specific probe achieves rho = 0.183, a ninefold improvement over the population probe (rho = 0.020, p < 10^-4). A negative control, fixation count, shows no person-specific advantage (p = 0.360); fixation count reflects word length and frequency rather than individual cognition. The individual directions are temporally stable (split-half cosine = 0.824), non-transferable across people (self rho = 0.369 vs. other rho = 0.143, p < 10^-19), and distinct from the shared population signal: person-specific probes retain predictive power after the population component is removed. The person-specific signal concentrates in the model's deep layers, rising consistently with depth and peaking at Layer 24 of 28. The results are consistent across architectures (LLaMA 3.1 8B) and survive word-level confound controls. Frozen language models contain stable, person-specific neural directions in their deep layers, providing a geometric foundation for EEG-driven personalization.

AI.tldr: Frozen LLMs encode person-specific EEG signals in their deep layers, enabling individual neural response prediction that outperforms population-level approaches by 9x.
AI.motivation: With consumer-grade EEG becoming ubiquitous in everyday devices, the paper investigates whether language models can be adapted to individual neural responses, specifically testing if frozen LLM representations contain person-specific EEG signals.
AI.method: Using word-level EEG data from 30 participants reading naturalistic sentences (ZuCo corpus), researchers trained separate linear probes for each person to map hidden states from frozen Qwen 2.5 7B to individual EEG power. They compared person-specific probes against population probes, tested temporal stability, cross-person transferability, and layer-wise analysis.
AI.result: Person-specific probes significantly outperformed population probes (rho = 0.183 vs 0.020 for high-gamma power, 9x improvement). The person-specific signal is temporally stable (cosine similarity = 0.824), non-transferable across individuals, distinct from population signal, and concentrates in deep layers (peaking at Layer 24/28). Results generalize across architectures (LLaMA 3.1 8B) and survive confound controls.
AI.conclusion: Frozen language models contain stable, person-specific neural directions in their deep layers, providing a geometric foundation for EEG-driven personalization of language models to individual cognitive responses.

title: Representation-Level Adversarial Regularization for Clinically Aligned Multitask Thyroid Ultrasound Assessment
summary: Thyroid ultrasound is the first-line exam for assessing thyroid nodules and determining whether biopsy is warranted. In routine reporting, radiologists produce two coupled outputs: a nodule contour for measurement and a TI-RADS risk category based on sonographic criteria. Yet both contouring style and risk grading vary across readers, creating inconsistent supervision that can degrade standard learning pipelines. In this paper, we address this workflow with a clinically guided multitask framework that jointly predicts the nodule mask and TI-RADS category within a single model. To ground risk prediction in clinically meaningful evidence, we guide the classification embedding using a compact TI-RADS aligned radiomics target during training, while preserving complementary deep features for discriminative performance. However, under annotator variability, naive multitask optimization often fails not because the tasks are unrelated, but because their gradients compete within the shared representation. To make this competition explicit and controllable, we introduce RLAR, a representation-level adversarial gradient regularizer. Rather than performing parameter-level gradient surgery, RLAR uses each task's normalized adversarial direction in latent space as a geometric probe of task sensitivity and penalizes excessive angular alignment between task-specific adversarial directions. On a public TI-RADS dataset, our clinically guided multitask model with RLAR consistently improves risk stratification while maintaining segmentation quality compared to single-task training and conventional multitask baselines. Code and pretrained models will be released.

AI.tldr: A multitask learning framework for thyroid ultrasound analysis that jointly predicts nodule segmentation and TI-RADS risk classification, using clinical guidance and adversarial gradient regularization to handle annotator variability and task competition.
AI.motivation: Thyroid ultrasound analysis suffers from inter-reader variability in both nodule contouring and TI-RADS risk grading, creating inconsistent supervision that degrades standard learning pipelines. There's a need for a unified approach that can handle both tasks while addressing the challenges of annotator variability and task competition in multitask learning.
AI.method: Proposes a clinically guided multitask framework that jointly predicts nodule mask and TI-RADS category. Uses TI-RADS-aligned radiomics targets to ground risk prediction in clinically meaningful evidence while preserving deep features. Introduces RLAR (Representation-Level Adversarial Regularizer) to explicitly manage gradient competition by penalizing excessive angular alignment between task-specific adversarial directions in latent space.
AI.result: On a public TI-RADS dataset, the clinically guided multitask model with RLAR consistently improves risk stratification while maintaining segmentation quality compared to single-task training and conventional multitask baselines.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses clinical workflow needs by jointly handling segmentation and risk classification, with RLAR providing explicit control over task competition to overcome limitations of naive multitask optimization under annotator variability.

title: Prompt replay: speeding up grpo with on-policy reuse of high-signal prompts
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) plays a crucial role in expanding the capacities of LLM reasoning, but GRPO-style training is dominated by expensive rollouts and wastes compute on unusable prompts. We propose Prompt Replay, an overhead-free online data selection method for GRPO that reuses prompts only (not trajectories), to preserve on-policy optimization. After each step, we insert prompts with medium difficulty into a buffer, and prioritize prompts closer to a pass rate of 0.5 (half answers correct, half wrong) to maximize the advantage, thus learning signal. Training batches are formed by mixing reused prompts with fresh samples, with cooldown steps and max reuse times controlling aggressiveness vs risk of overfitting. Across multiple model families (Llama-3.2- 3B, Qwen3-8B) and training datasets (Dolci, Polaris), evaluated using average accuracy on six standard math benchmarks, Prompt Replay reduces zero-variance prompts, increases mean absolute advantage and shows faster initial accuracy gains. Yet, it plateaus and converges with the baseline, as too aggressive configuration was used. The method is most efficient when the rollouts are the primary bottleneck and the dataset is difficult for the model. We additionally observe that Qwen2.5-Math can exhibit spurious-reward effects that invalidates ablations, raising a warning signal for using it as a sole testbed for GRPO method research.

AI.tldr: Prompt Replay is an online data selection method for GRPO-style RLVR that reuses prompts (not trajectories) to reduce rollout costs while maintaining on-policy optimization, showing faster initial accuracy gains but converging with baseline due to aggressive configuration.
AI.motivation: GRPO-style reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) is computationally expensive due to costly rollouts and wastes compute on unusable prompts, creating a need for more efficient training methods.
AI.method: Prompt Replay reuses only prompts (not trajectories) in an overhead-free online data selection approach. It maintains a buffer of prompts with medium difficulty, prioritizing those closer to a 50% pass rate to maximize learning signal. Training batches mix reused prompts with fresh samples, with cooldown steps and max reuse times controlling aggressiveness vs overfitting risk.
AI.result: Across multiple model families (Llama-3.2-3B, Qwen3-8B) and training datasets (Dolci, Polaris), Prompt Replay reduces zero-variance prompts, increases mean absolute advantage, and shows faster initial accuracy gains on six standard math benchmarks. However, it plateaus and converges with baseline due to overly aggressive configuration. Most effective when rollouts are the primary bottleneck and dataset is difficult for the model.
AI.conclusion: Prompt Replay offers an efficient data selection method for GRPO-style RLVR that reduces rollout costs while preserving on-policy optimization, though configuration must be carefully tuned to avoid premature convergence. The paper also warns about spurious-reward effects in Qwen2.5-Math that can invalidate ablations.

title: GSEM: Graph-based Self-Evolving Memory for Experience Augmented Clinical Reasoning
summary: Clinical decision-making agents can benefit from reusing prior decision experience. However, many memory-augmented methods store experiences as independent records without explicit relational structure, which may introduce noisy retrieval, unreliable reuse, and in some cases even hurt performance compared to direct LLM inference. We propose GSEM (Graph-based Self-Evolving Memory), a clinical memory framework that organizes clinical experiences into a dual-layer memory graph, capturing both the decision structure within each experience and the relational dependencies across experiences, and supporting applicability-aware retrieval and online feedback-driven calibration of node quality and edge weights. Across MedR-Bench and MedAgentsBench with two LLM backbones, GSEM achieves the highest average accuracy among all baselines, reaching 70.90\% and 69.24\% with DeepSeek-V3.2 and Qwen3.5-35B, respectively. Code is available at https://github.com/xhan1022/gsem.

AI.tldr: GSEM is a graph-based clinical memory framework that organizes decision experiences into a dual-layer graph structure to improve retrieval and reuse, outperforming baseline methods on medical benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing memory-augmented methods store clinical experiences as independent records without explicit relational structure, leading to noisy retrieval, unreliable reuse, and sometimes worse performance than direct LLM inference.
AI.method: GSEM organizes clinical experiences into a dual-layer memory graph that captures both decision structure within each experience and relational dependencies across experiences, with applicability-aware retrieval and online feedback-driven calibration of node quality and edge weights.
AI.result: GSEM achieves the highest average accuracy among all baselines on MedR-Bench and MedAgentsBench, reaching 70.90% with DeepSeek-V3.2 and 69.24% with Qwen3.5-35B.
AI.conclusion: The graph-based organization of clinical experiences with explicit relational structure improves retrieval quality and decision reuse, enabling better clinical decision-making compared to independent memory storage approaches.

title: Ara-Best-RQ: Multi Dialectal Arabic SSL
summary: We present Ara-BEST-RQ, a family of self-supervised learning (SSL) models specifically designed for multi-dialectal Arabic speech processing. Leveraging 5,640 hours of crawled Creative Commons speech and combining it with publicly available datasets, we pre-train conformer-based BEST-RQ models up to 600M parameters. Our models are evaluated on dialect identification (DID) and automatic speech recognition (ASR) tasks, achieving state-of-the-art performance on the former while using fewer parameters than competing models. We demonstrate that family-targeted pre-training on Arabic dialects significantly improves downstream performance compared to multilingual or monolingual models trained on non-Arabic data. All models, code, and pre-processed datasets will be publicly released to support reproducibility and further research in Arabic speech technologies.

AI.tldr: Ara-BEST-RQ is a family of self-supervised speech models for multi-dialectal Arabic, achieving state-of-the-art dialect identification performance with 600M parameter conformer-based architectures trained on 5,640+ hours of Arabic speech data.
AI.motivation: There is a need for specialized self-supervised learning models for multi-dialectal Arabic speech processing, as existing multilingual or monolingual models trained on non-Arabic data may not adequately capture the linguistic diversity and characteristics of Arabic dialects.
AI.method: The authors pre-train conformer-based BEST-RQ models up to 600M parameters using 5,640 hours of crawled Creative Commons speech combined with publicly available datasets. The approach involves family-targeted pre-training specifically on Arabic dialects rather than general multilingual or monolingual training.
AI.result: The models achieve state-of-the-art performance on dialect identification (DID) tasks while using fewer parameters than competing models. They also demonstrate strong performance on automatic speech recognition (ASR) tasks. The family-targeted pre-training on Arabic dialects significantly improves downstream performance compared to alternative approaches.
AI.conclusion: Specialized pre-training on Arabic dialects yields superior performance for Arabic speech processing tasks compared to general multilingual or monolingual approaches. The public release of models, code, and pre-processed datasets will support reproducibility and advance research in Arabic speech technologies.

title: Learning Progressive Adaptation for Multi-Modal Tracking
summary: Due to the limited availability of paired multi-modal data, multi-modal trackers are typically built by adopting pre-trained RGB models with parameter-efficient fine-tuning modules. However, these fine-tuning methods overlook advanced adaptations for applying RGB pre-trained models and fail to modulate a single specific modality, cross-modal interactions, and the prediction head. To address the issues, we propose to perform Progressive Adaptation for Multi-Modal Tracking (PATrack). This innovative approach incorporates modality-dependent, modality-entangled, and task-level adapters, effectively bridging the gap in adapting RGB pre-trained networks to multi-modal data through a progressive strategy. Specifically, modality-specific information is enhanced through the modality-dependent adapter, decomposing the high- and low-frequency components, which ensures a more robust feature representation within each modality. The inter-modal interactions are introduced in the modality-entangled adapter, which implements a cross-attention operation guided by inter-modal shared information, ensuring the reliability of features conveyed between modalities. Additionally, recognising that the strong inductive bias of the prediction head does not adapt to the fused information, a task-level adapter specific to the prediction head is introduced. In summary, our design integrates intra-modal, inter-modal, and task-level adapters into a unified framework. Extensive experiments on RGB+Thermal, RGB+Depth, and RGB+Event tracking tasks demonstrate that our method shows impressive performance against state-of-the-art methods. Code is available at https://github.com/ouha1998/Learning-Progressive-Adaptation-for-Multi-Modal-Tracking.

AI.tldr: PATrack proposes progressive adaptation with modality-dependent, modality-entangled, and task-level adapters to bridge RGB pre-trained models to multi-modal tracking, addressing limitations of existing fine-tuning methods.
AI.motivation: Existing fine-tuning methods for multi-modal tracking overlook advanced adaptations for RGB pre-trained models and fail to modulate single modalities, cross-modal interactions, and prediction heads, limiting performance with limited paired multi-modal data.
AI.method: PATrack introduces progressive adaptation with three adapter types: modality-dependent adapter (enhances single modalities via high/low-frequency decomposition), modality-entangled adapter (facilitates cross-modal interactions via cross-attention), and task-level adapter (adapts prediction head to fused information).
AI.result: Extensive experiments on RGB+Thermal, RGB+Depth, and RGB+Event tracking tasks demonstrate impressive performance against state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: PATrack effectively bridges RGB pre-trained networks to multi-modal data through progressive adaptation, integrating intra-modal, inter-modal, and task-level adapters into a unified framework for superior multi-modal tracking.
